Article.ArticleTitle: SGLT2 inhibitors and mortality in older adults with diabetic kidney disease: A target trial emulation study.

Abstract.AbstractText: Randomized controlled trials on sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors have yielded inconsistent findings regarding mortality benefits. It remains unclear whether, and in which subgroups, SGLT2 inhibitors confer survival benefits in older adults with diabetic kidney disease (DKD).

We emulated a target trial using a nationwide claims and health checkup database in Japan to compare all-cause mortality between SGLT2 and dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP4) inhibitor initiators among 5371 adults aged ≥65 years with DKD. The primary outcome was all-cause mortality. Hazard ratios (HRs) were estimated using propensity score overlap weighting and Cox proportional hazards models in an intention-to-treat analysis. A per-protocol analysis with inverse probability of censoring weighting was conducted as sensitivity analysis. Effect modification by age, body mass index (BMI) and Charlson comorbidity index (CCI) was assessed using restricted cubic spline models.

During a median follow-up of 2.23 (IQR, 1.07-3.49) years, 437 deaths occurred. SGLT2 inhibitor use was associated with significantly lower all-cause mortality than DPP4 inhibitors (HR, 0.51; 95% confidence interval, 0.38-0.70), with consistent results in the per-protocol analysis (HR, 0.50; 95% confidence interval, 0.35-0.73). Survival benefit was evident up to about 80 years of age and among individuals with BMI ≥22 kg/m2, irrespective of CCI.

In this target trial emulation study, SGLT2 inhibitors were associated with lower mortality in older adults with DKD, particularly those under 80 years and with BMI ≥22 kg/m2. These findings support personalized treatment decisions for this high-risk population in clinical practice.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Hidehiro Kaneko and Katsuhiko Fujiu have received research funding and scholarship funds from Medtronic Japan, Boston Scientific Japan, BIOTRONIK Japan, and Simplex Quantum. Hidehiro Kaneko holds shares in PrevMed and Japan Preventive Medicine Development Institute. Toshiyuki Ko is a member of the Cardiovascular Medicine cooperative program between the University of Tokyo and Boehringer Ingelheim and has received research grants from Daiichi Sankyo. Norihiko Takeda has received collaborative research funding from AstraZeneca and Ono Pharmaceutical. Koichi Node has received honoraria from Boehringer Ingelheim, research grants from Astellas and scholarship funds from Daiichi Sankyo and Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma. Kaori Hayashi has received scholarship funds from Boehringer Ingelheim. Takashi Yokoo has received honoraria for lectures from Boehringer Ingelheim, Eli Lilly Japan and AstraZeneca. Masaomi Nangaku has received scholarship funds from Boehringer Ingelheim.
